items.front: What key limitation distinguishes PDC from other automated departure-clearance methods?
items.back: PDC may not transmit amended or revised flight plans. Those clearances must be issued verbally, and any PDC sent still has to be accurate, complete, continuous, and operational in nature.

items.front: Can PDC transmit amended or revised flight plans?
items.back: NO. PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans — they must be verbally issued. A flight plan with automation-assigned route (e.g., ADR) is not considered amended. Per 14-1-1(d).

items.front: What key capability does CPDLC DCL have that PDC does not?
items.back: CPDLC DCL may transmit amended or revised flight plans. If a revision cannot be delivered by CPDLC, issue it verbally, and ensure the clearance is accurate, complete, and continuous.

items.front: If no SID is assigned and there is no SID altitude or vertical guidance, what must the DCL selectable fields show?
items.back: Use NO SID in Field 1 if none is assigned, ---- in Field 2 if no transition is assigned, and MAINTAIN (altitude) in Field 5 when there is no SID altitude or vertical guidance.

items.front: How should a domestic CPDLC weather-deviation request be handled if the aircraft still has climb-via, descend-via, or crossing restrictions?
items.back: Unable the request via CPDLC and revert to voice communications. Also ensure no trajectory-altering clearances remain open before transfer unless otherwise coordinated.

items.front: How should domestic CPDLC message tables be used relative to urgent voice clearances?
items.back: They define the approved CPDLC message set, but immediate or expeditious clearances should not use CPDLC unless voice is not operationally feasible.

items.front: When should CPDLC NOT be used?
items.back:
1. During critical phases of flight
2. For immediate/expeditious clearances (unless voice not feasible)
3. When there are open trajectory altering clearances. Per 14-2-1(c-e).

items.front: If a CPDLC clearance is being overridden by voice and it contained multiple elements, what must the controller do?
items.back: Use voice and restate the entire CPDLC clearance. Acknowledge abnormal indications only after required coordination, and do not cancel an uplink before the pilot acknowledges the superseding voice clearance.

items.front: What voice action is required when domestic CPDLC service shuts down or fails?
items.back: Broadcast by voice that CPDLC is no longer in use, request no pilot downlinks until further advised, and resolve open or abnormally closed uplinks with each aircraft by voice.

items.front: Which domestic CPDLC uplink uses are prohibited or limited?
items.back: Do not use advisory or free text for control instructions needing acknowledgment. Do not use CPDLC for speed changes on procedures with published speed restrictions or to clear aircraft out of holding.

items.front: When oceanic CPDLC is available and a connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage, how should communication be handled?
items.back: Use CPDLC as the primary means, keep a voice backup available, reply by CPDLC when the pilot uses CPDLC, and use standard message-set elements instead of free text when possible.

items.front: Why does oceanic CPDLC guidance prefer the message set over free text?
items.back: The message set supports proper closure of CPDLC exchanges; free text should be avoided to the extent possible.

items.front: How is oceanic transfer of communications handled when the next facility is or is not CPDLC capable?
items.back: If the receiving facility is CPDLC capable, transfer is automatic within adapted parameters. If not, transfer communications under local directives and LOAs.

items.front: What should the controller do if oceanic automated data-link transfer fails or clearance delivery by CPDLC is uncertain?
items.back: Attempt a manual transfer. If still unable, tell the pilot to log on to the next facility and send EOS. If delivery of a clearance is uncertain, keep protecting that airspace until the crew responds.
